At 1.5 meters depth in the eastern pond, excavators found a beautifully preserved sandstone turtle sculpture measuring 57cm × 43cm × 21cm. In Hindu cosmology, the turtle is fundamental to creation myths, and perhaps had other virtues in Khmer mythology, with many found appearing to be sacred deposits.  The turtle’s discovery confirmed what the excavations had been suggesting (as noted earlier): Bayon

title: Statue Fragments Found Near Cambodia's Bayon Temple
highlights:
large statue fragments have been recovered from a canal near the Gate of the Dead at Angkor Thom by members of Cambodia’s Department of Monuments and Preventive Archaeology, the heritage police, and agents from the Apsara Authority. “The god statue found by the working team has four pieces,  ...    and Preventive Archaeology. The pieces are thought to have come from a sculpture depicting the Hindu story of the Churning of the Milk Ocean, in which the semi-divine half-human, half-serpent who lived on Shiva’s neck was used as a churning rope wrapped around Mount Mandara. The gods held one end of the naga, while demons held the other. Since the Bayon’s rediscovery by French scholars and explorers in the 19th century, the identity of the smiling figures that greet visitors to the temple and to the city of Angkor Thom has been debated.
